title: Endocrine Emergencies — Hypoglycemia, DKA & HHS
learningObjectives: Recognize the field presentation of hypoglycemia and describe its treatment.
Describe the diagnostic framework for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
Describe the diagnostic framework for hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS).
Differentiate DKA from HHS on field-relevant grounds.
Explain euglycemic DKA and why a normal glucose does not rule out DKA.

summary.title: Hypoglycemia — Fast to Recognize, Fast to Fix
summary.content: Hypoglycemia presents with a spectrum from mild symptoms (shakiness, diaphoresis, hunger, anxiety) to severe presentations (confusion, combativeness, seizure, unresponsiveness) as glucose falls further. It is common, rapidly correctable, and a well-known mimic of stroke, seizure, and behavioral emergencies — which is why a glucose check is a near-universal first step for any altered patient. Field treatment is oral glucose in an awake, cooperative patient with intact airway reflexes, or IV dextrose / IM glucagon per protocol for a patient who cannot safely take oral glucose.

summary.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
summary.content: DKA is a hyperglycemic emergency defined by three features together: hyperglycemia (glucose ≥200 mg/dL, or a prior history of diabetes even without meeting this threshold), ketosis (elevated ketones — beta-hydroxybutyrate ≥3.0 mmol/L or urine ketones 2+ or greater), and metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3 and/or bicarbonate <18 mEq/L). Classic field findings include polyuria/polydipsia leading up to the event, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, deep rapid (Kussmaul) respirations as a compensatory response to the acidosis, a fruity (acetone) breath odor, and dehydration. DKA is most classically associated with type 1 diabetes but can occur in type 2 diabetes as well, often precipitated by infection, missed insulin doses, or new-onset diabetes.

summary.title: Euglycemic DKA
summary.content: Approximately 10% of DKA presentations occur with a glucose under 200 mg/dL — euglycemic DKA — most classically associated with SGLT2-inhibitor diabetes medications, but also seen with reduced oral intake, pregnancy, or partial insulin treatment before presentation. This means a normal or only mildly elevated glucose does not rule out DKA if the clinical picture otherwise fits (Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, vomiting/abdominal pain, known diabetes) — glucose alone is an incomplete screening tool for this condition.

summary.title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
summary.content: HHS is a hyperglycemic emergency defined by severe hyperglycemia (often glucose >600 mg/dL), significant hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg), minimal or absent ketosis, and near-normal pH/bicarbonate — distinguishing it from DKA's acidosis-driven picture. HHS is more typically seen in older adults with type 2 diabetes, often with a slower onset over days, profound dehydration, and a more prominent altered-mental-status/obtundation picture than DKA, reflecting the severe hyperosmolar state's direct effect on the brain. Some patients present with mixed features of both DKA and HHS.

summary.title: Field Management
summary.content: For both DKA and HHS: supportive care (oxygen as needed, IV access and isotonic fluids per protocol, cardiac monitoring), careful attention to mental status and airway protection given the risk of vomiting, and rapid transport — definitive treatment (insulin, careful fluid/electrolyte correction) happens in the hospital. Field providers should always check glucose in any patient with altered mental status, vomiting, abdominal pain, or unexplained rapid breathing, since DKA/HHS can mimic other conditions and the reverse is also true (do not assume every diabetic patient with altered mental status is hypoglycemic without checking).

clinicalPearls: Hypoglycemia is common, fast, and correctable — check glucose early in any altered patient.
DKA = hyperglycemia (or known diabetes) + ketosis + acidosis, together — not any one feature alone.
About 10% of DKA is euglycemic (glucose <200 mg/dL) — don't rule out DKA on a normal glucose alone if the clinical picture fits.
HHS = severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolality + minimal ketosis + near-normal pH — a different mechanism than DKA's acidosis.
redFlags: Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath odor, vomiting/abdominal pain in a known or new diabetic patient — think DKA even with a near-normal glucose.
Profound dehydration with severe altered mental status/obtundation in an older type 2 diabetic — think HHS.
Any diabetic patient with altered mental status — check glucose rather than assuming the direction of the abnormality.
commonMistakes: Ruling out DKA because the glucose is 'not that high,' without considering euglycemic DKA.
Assuming every altered diabetic patient is hypoglycemic without checking glucose.
Treating DKA/HHS field management as identical to a simple hypoglycemia treat-and-reassess — both need IV fluids and rapid transport, not just glucose correction.
memoryAids: DKA: hyperglycemia + ketosis + acidosis, all together.
HHS: sky-high glucose, sky-high osmolality, minimal ketones.
10% of DKA hides behind a normal-ish glucose — euglycemic DKA is real.

decisionTrees.title: Suspected Diabetic Emergency Field Approach
decisionTrees.content:
Altered mental status, vomiting, abdominal pain, or unexplained rapid breathing in a known or possible diabetic
  → Check blood glucose immediately
  → Low glucose → treat hypoglycemia per protocol (oral glucose if awake/cooperative; IV dextrose or IM glucagon per protocol if not) → reassess
  → High or near-normal glucose with Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, vomiting/abdominal pain, dehydration → suspect DKA (consider euglycemic DKA even if glucose is only mildly elevated) → supportive care, IV fluids per protocol, rapid transport
  → Very high glucose with severe dehydration, minimal respiratory compensation, and profound altered mental status in an older type 2 diabetic → suspect HHS → supportive care, IV fluids per protocol, rapid transport
  → All suspected DKA/HHS: monitor airway closely given vomiting risk, transport for definitive hospital treatment

references: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026, Section 16 (Diabetes Care in the Hospital)
2024 ADA Consensus Report: Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes
